skill.name: claude-code-debug
skill.description: Diagnose Claude Code skills, hooks, agents, MCP connections, permissions, and configuration issues with command references and troubleshooting flows.
skill.summary: Troubleshoot Claude Code extension loading, hook execution, agent discovery, MCP setup, and permission issues.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, environment, and URL patterns, but review shows they are Markdown troubleshooting examples rather than executable package code. The skill is not malicious, but it can guide users to inspect Claude settings, session files, and token-related environment variables, so publication should include a warning about sensitive local data.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Local Configuration and Session Inspection
medium_findings.description: The skill documents commands that read Claude settings, project MCP configuration, permission rules, and recent session files. This is useful for debugging, but users may expose secrets or private conversation context if they paste outputs into a chat or ticket.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly inspect settings, permissions, MCP files, and session files. The context is legitimate troubleshooting, but these files can contain sensitive local data.

medium_findings.title: Environment Token Disclosure in Debug Commands
medium_findings.description: The references include commands that print token-related environment variables and an API key prefix. These examples can reveal secret presence or partial secret values during support workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in diagnostic documentation and target token-related environment data. They do not transmit data externally, so the risk depends on user handling of output.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell Diagnostic Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides many shell commands, including chmod, npx, npm, just, jq, grep, cat, and Claude CLI invocations. They are examples rather than hidden execution, but the skill requires Bash access and users should review commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are clear documentation examples tied to the debugging purpose. Risk is moderate because they can alter permissions or run external packages if executed without review.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the reviewed lines are ordinary prose, Markdown separators, or Claude Code debugging text. No cryptographic implementation or hash usage was found in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto code at the cited locations. The hits appear to come from generic pattern matching over documentation text.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Without Data Transfer
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to official Claude Code documentation pages. No evidence found that the skill sends local data to these URLs or performs network requests automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain reference links in a documentation section. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or network automation around them.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Hits Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: The many external command findings are located in fenced blocks, tables, and troubleshooting flows. They are not hidden scripts or install-time hooks, but they still warrant user review before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is command-reference documentation and checklist text. No evidence found of automatic command execution outside user-requested debugging.

dangerous_patterns.title: Commands That Read Sensitive Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Diagnostic examples read Claude settings, MCP configuration, permission rules, and session files. Users should redact sensitive values before sharing outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly read local configuration and session paths. The pattern is expected for debugging, but the files can include sensitive information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Commands That Reveal Token Environment Data
dangerous_patterns.description: Diagnostic examples include token searches and API key prefix output. This can disclose partial credentials or confirm sensitive environment variable names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the documentation. It does not exfiltrate data by itself, but output sharing would create exposure.

content.value_statement: Claude Code extensions can fail because of misplaced files, invalid settings, missing permissions, or unclear triggers. This skill provides focused checks and troubleshooting flows for skills, hooks, agents, MCP servers, and configuration problems.
actual_capabilities: Guides diagnosis of Claude Code skill activation problems.
Provides checks for hook registration, execution, and exit behavior.
Lists commands for inspecting agents, memory files, permissions, and configuration.
Explains MCP server connection checks and environment variable validation.
Offers decision-tree flows for extension, hook, agent, command, MCP, and permission issues.
Links to official Claude Code documentation for related concepts.
limitations: It is a troubleshooting guide, not an automated repair tool.
Some commands may reveal local settings, sessions, or token names.
It assumes Claude Code CLI conventions and may not match older versions.
It does not validate custom scripts for security or correctness.

use_cases.title: Fix a Skill That Does Not Activate
use_cases.description: Check directory names, SKILL.md casing, frontmatter, and trigger wording when a custom skill is ignored.

use_cases.title: Debug a Hook That Does Not Run
use_cases.description: Verify executable permissions, settings JSON, matcher names, script paths, and manual hook execution.

use_cases.title: Investigate MCP and Permission Problems
use_cases.description: Review MCP server setup, environment variables, allow rules, deny rules, and debug output for connection failures.

prompt_templates.title: Start Basic Diagnostics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for an extension issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use claude-code-debug to help me diagnose why my Claude Code extension is not working. Start with safe read-only checks.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Skill Loading
prompt_templates.scenario: A custom skill does not activate.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use claude-code-debug to inspect a skill that is not loading. Check the folder name, SKILL.md file, frontmatter, and description triggers.

prompt_templates.title: Review Hook Execution
prompt_templates.scenario: A configured hook is not firing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use claude-code-debug to troubleshoot my Claude Code hook. Verify the matcher, settings JSON, executable bit, path, and manual test result.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze MCP and Permission Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use claude-code-debug to build a diagnosis plan for MCP connection failures and permission denials. Avoid printing secrets or full session files.

output_examples.input: My custom skill does not activate when I ask for it.
output_examples.output: The response identifies likely causes, such as a mismatched directory name, missing SKILL.md file, invalid frontmatter, or weak trigger wording. It recommends checking each item in order and using debug mode if the structure is correct.

output_examples.input: A PreToolUse hook is configured but never blocks anything.
output_examples.output: The response walks through executable permissions, matcher casing, settings JSON validity, script path resolution, manual script testing, and the expected blocking exit code.

output_examples.input: My MCP server does not appear in Claude Code.
output_examples.output: The response separates package installation, server startup, MCP configuration, transport mode, and environment variable checks. It warns the user to redact token values before sharing command output.

best_practices: Run read-only inspection commands before changing files or permissions.
Redact tokens, session content, and private paths before sharing output.
Use Claude debug output with a specific symptom and one failing extension at a time.
anti_patterns: Do not paste full settings, session logs, or environment output into public channels.
Do not run npm, npx, or hook scripts from unknown projects without review.
Do not broaden permission allow rules until you understand the denied tool pattern.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides troubleshooting commands and flows for the assistant to use during debugging with user oversight.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it includes commands that can display settings, sessions, token names, and partial API key values.

faq.question: Can it fix my Claude Code configuration?
faq.answer: It can guide diagnosis and suggest changes. You should review each proposed command or edit before applying it.

faq.question: What issues does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers skill loading, hook execution, agent discovery, MCP server setup, permission decisions, memory files, and validation commands.

faq.question: Should I share command output with others?
faq.answer: Share only the minimum needed output. Remove tokens, private paths, session text, and project-specific secrets first.

faq.question: Does it support Codex as well as Claude Code?
faq.answer: The content is written for Claude Code behavior, but Codex users can use it to inspect compatible skill files and debugging patterns.
